input.user_input: Explain the notice-and-comment rulemaking process under the Administrative Procedure Act.

actual:
The notice-and-comment rulemaking process, often referred to as "informal rulemaking," is the primary method by which federal agencies create, amend, or repeal administrative regulations. Governed by Section 553 of the Administrative Procedure Act (APA) (5 U.S.C. § 553), this process is designed to ensure transparency, public participation, and reasoned decision-making in the creation of federal regulations.

Here is a step-by-step breakdown of the notice-and-comment rulemaking process:

**1. Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM)**
When an agency decides to issue a new rule or modify an existing one, it must first publish a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking in the Federal Register. Under the APA, this notice must include:
* A statement of the time, place, and nature of the public rulemaking proceedings.
* Reference to the specific legal authority under which the rule is proposed (usually a statute passed by Congress).
* Either the exact text of the proposed rule or a detailed description of the subjects and issues
